refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation communication path. Validate that loss remains ≤1% (or customer target) during continuous operation and transient network stress scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert when packet loss exceeds the customer-defined threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive bitrate encoding and forward error correction (FEC) to recover lost packets without retransmission.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to protect teleoperation packets from congestion-induced loss.
DP-D: Combine DP-A, DP-B, and DP-C in a layered architecture: redundancy at transport, FEC at codec, QoS at network.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a layered packet loss mitigation architecture combining redundant communication channels (dual network paths with automatic failover), forward error correction at the codec layer, and QoS-based traffic prioritization. This multi-layer approach addresses packet loss at transport, application, and network levels, providing defense-in-depth suitable for safety-critical teleoperation.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation system design (codec, network stack) with network infrastructure provisioning (QoS, dual paths). This is acceptable because both are within the NGHE solution scope and must be co-designed.
Redundant channels couple with operator interface (must display active/standby status). Manageable via clear interface contracts.
FEC couples with bandwidth budget; must be validated against available network capacity.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is moderate and intentional. The layered approach avoids over-reliance on any single mitigation technique, reducing risk of single-point failure in packet loss strategy.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and well-characterized (latency, jitter, available bandwidth, typical loss baseline).
ASSUMPTION: Dual network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual ISP) are available at the worksite.
RISK: If network conditions are highly variable or unpredictable, FEC overhead may be insufficient or excessive, requiring dynamic tuning.
RISK: Redundant channels increase power consumption and cost; trade-off must be validated against customer budget and operational constraints.
RISK: Codec-level FEC introduces latency; must verify that added latency does not degrade operator control responsiveness below acceptable thresholds.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC algorithm and overhead budget (e.g., Reed-Solomon with 10–20% redundancy), (2) QoS marking scheme (e.g., DSCP for teleoperation vs. telemetry), (3) failover detection and switchover time target (e.g., <100 ms), and (4) monitoring/alerting thresholds for packet loss trending. Add a sub-DP for dynamic adaptation: if measured loss approaches threshold, increase FEC overhead or reduce video bitrate.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication pipeline, including: (1) command encoding/decoding and transmission, (2) feedback (video, telemetry) reception and decoding, (3) codec selection and FEC implementation, (4) QoS marking and traffic prioritization, (5) redundant channel management and failover logic, (6) packet loss monitoring and reporting. The module interfaces with the Network Link (transport), Safety Services (loss alerts), and the Vehicle (command/feedback endpoints).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel(s) to the vehicle. Teleoperation module sends encoded commands and receives encoded feedback over one or more network paths. Network Link provides raw packet transport; Teleoperation adds FEC, QoS marking, and redundancy logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (e.g., move forward, rotate, lift) are received by Teleoperation module from the operator interface. Tasking is encoded and transmitted via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Feedback data (video, sensor readings, equipment state) is received by Teleoperation module from the Vehicle via Network Link, decoded, and forwarded as Telemetry to the operator interface and monitoring systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: If packet loss exceeds the customer-defined threshold, Teleoperation module generates a SafetyAlert to notify the Safety Services module and operator, triggering graceful degradation or emergency stop logic.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse packet loss mitigation logic across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, Network). Not recommended; too coarse-grained.

Vehicle module owns the equipment hardware and local control. Packet loss mitigation (FEC, QoS, redundancy) is primarily a communication/software concern, not a vehicle hardware concern. Assigning to Vehicle would diffuse network-layer logic into equipment control, violating separation of concerns.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end teleoperation communication quality. It encapsulates codec, FEC, redundancy management, QoS, and packet loss monitoring. All DP-D sub-components (redundant channels, FEC, QoS, failover) are cohesive within this module. Minimal diffusion; high containment.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerting. Packet loss monitoring and SafetyAlert generation are safety-relevant, but the technical implementation of FEC, redundancy, and QoS belongs in Teleoperation. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, not own the mitigation logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D because: (1) it encapsulates all teleoperation communication logic (codec, FEC, redundancy, QoS), (2) it has clear ownership of the Tasking input and Telemetry output interfaces, (3) it can monitor packet loss and generate SafetyAlert without diffusion, (4) it maintains cohesion with the Network Link interface, and (5) it avoids scattering network-layer logic across Vehicle or Safety Services.
containment_risks: RISK: If the Vehicle module has local packet loss detection (e.g., at the radio transceiver), coordination between Vehicle and Teleoperation packet loss reporting must be explicit. Recommend clear interface contract for loss metrics.

RISK: QoS marking (e.g., DSCP) may require coordination with network infrastructure provisioning, which may be outside Teleoperation's direct control. Recommend that Teleoperation specifies QoS requirements and Safety Services/Project Manager ensures network provisioning.

RISK: Failover logic (switching between dual network paths) must be fast and deterministic. If failover is slow, packet loss during switchover may exceed threshold. Recommend explicit latency budget and testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation and Network Link: Teleoperation owns FEC, QoS marking, and redundancy logic; Network Link owns raw packet transport and physical channel management. Define explicit interface contracts for: (1) packet format (FEC-encoded payload, QoS marking), (2) loss metrics reporting (per-channel loss, aggregate loss), and (3) failover signaling (active/standby channel status). Add a sub-module or component within Teleoperation for 'Redundancy Manager' to own failover logic and dual-channel coordination.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (layered packet loss mitigation) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (maintain packet loss ≤1%). The multi-layer approach (redundancy, FEC, QoS) is well-motivated by the safety-critical nature of teleoperation and the need for defense-in-depth. DP-D is implementable and verifiable. No decoupling issues detected.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: QoS provisioning requires coordination with network infrastructure (outside Teleoperation module). Mitigate by defining clear QoS requirements specification and ensuring network team commitment.

Low risk: Failover logic couples Teleoperation with dual network paths. Mitigate by encapsulating failover in a dedicated Redundancy Manager sub-component with clear state machine and interface contracts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) for DP-D. All technical components (FEC, redundancy, QoS, monitoring) are cohesive and owned by Teleoperation. External dependencies (Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined interfaces. Containment is acceptable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) Packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions is straightforward (packet capture, loss calculation). (2) FEC effectiveness can be validated via controlled loss injection. (3) Failover time can be measured via network simulation. (4) QoS marking can be verified via packet inspection. Recommend test plan with scenarios: baseline loss, transient congestion, channel failure, and combined stress.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns packet loss mitigation; Safety Services owns SafetyAlert response; Network Link owns transport. Responsibilities are well-separated. Operator interface owns display of packet loss metrics and failover status.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify FEC algorithm, overhead budget, and dynamic adaptation logic (as noted in selected_dp section).

Define explicit interface contracts for: (1) packet format (FEC-encoded, QoS-marked), (2) loss metrics (per-channel, aggregate, trend), (3) failover signaling (active/standby status, switchover latency).
Add a test plan for packet loss verification, including scenarios for baseline loss, transient congestion, channel failure, and combined stress under agreed network conditions.
Clarify network infrastructure provisioning requirements (dual paths, QoS support) and ensure customer/network team commitment.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (error detection/recovery, monitoring/reporting, graceful degradation). DP-D is well-selected and assigned to Teleoperation with high containment. However, to ensure complete coverage and traceability, the three sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level, each with its own DP and IM assignment. This ensures that error detection, monitoring, and degradation logic are explicitly designed and allocated.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert when packet loss exceeds the customer-defined threshold.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and well-characterized.
Dual network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite) are available at the worksite.
Operator interface can display packet loss metrics and failover status.
Safety Services module can receive and act on SafetyAlert from Teleoperation.
Network infrastructure supports QoS marking (e.g., DSCP).
open_risks: Network conditions may be highly variable; FEC overhead tuning may be required dynamically.
Failover latency during channel switchover may cause transient packet loss spike; must be validated against threshold.
QoS provisioning is outside Teleoperation's direct control; requires network team commitment.
Codec-level FEC introduces latency; must verify operator control responsiveness is acceptable.
evidence_needed: Customer-agreed network conditions specification (latency, jitter, bandwidth, baseline loss).
Dual network path availability and provisioning plan.
FEC algorithm selection and overhead budget validation (via simulation or prototype).
Failover detection and switchover time measurement (target: <100 ms).
Operator control latency budget and validation (e.g., <500 ms end-to-end).
Test plan for packet loss verification under agreed conditions and stress scenarios.
